About Me

A little more about me.

I’m John Li, a Mechanical Engineering student from Brooklyn, New York. I graduated from Stony Brook University in May 2026 with a 3.8 GPA, Dean’s List honors, and University Scholar distinction — placing me in the top 8% of my class. I’m continuing at Carnegie Mellon University for an MS in Mechanical Engineering, concentrating in Design and Manufacturing.

My background spans structural FEA for nuclear-adjacent lab facilities at Brookhaven National Laboratory, NASA-funded composite fracture mechanics research, and leading a multi-team rocket program from PDR through competition launch. I know how to take a problem from first principles to a physical result.

Projects

Engineering in action.

01

Battle of the Rockets — Two-Vehicle Campaign

Stony Brook University · Vehicle & Propulsion Subteam Lead

Led design, fabrication, and launch of two recoverable high-power competition rockets for the 2026 FGE Battle of the Rockets in Culpeper, VA, finishing 4th overall. Mars Rover vehicle achieved 1,459 ft apogee with successful rover deployment; Altitude vehicle reached 758 ft targeting 801 ft. Both vehicles recovered 100% intact.

SolidWorksRockSimMATLABCFDAvionicsFlight SimulationRecovery Systems
02

Precision Aluminum Car — Manual Machining

Stony Brook University · Manufacturing Processes

Designed and fully machined a miniature aluminum car from raw stock using manual mills and lathes. Every feature — the slotted chassis, cylindrical axle bosses, and set-screw retention — was produced to dimensional tolerance without CNC assistance. Engraved “JOHN 24” marking the year of manufacture.

Manual MillingLathe OperationsAluminum MachiningTolerancingFixturingProcess Planning

Research

Advancing materials science.

Composites · NASA Space Grant · Dr. Kedar Kirane · SBU Materials & Mechanics Lab

Compressive Failure of Woven FRP Composites Under Open Hole Compression

Investigating how hole size affects compressive strength in woven fiber-reinforced polymer laminates used in aerospace, automotive, and naval structures. ASTM D6484 OHC tests on hole sizes 3–6 mm reveal fracture process zone (FPZ) effects that plateau strength beyond ~6 mm. Sub-scale specimens converged to 42–46 MPa first-damage gross stress regardless of width, indicating a load-introduction-limited failure mode.

ASTM D6484OHC TestingWoven FRP LaminatesShimadzu UTMOptical MicrographyFPZ AnalysisHigh-Speed Imaging
